sections.title: Singular Pronouns
sections.explanation: Arabic has more personal pronouns than English because it distinguishes gender in the second and third person. The singular pronouns are: أنا (anā, I), أنتَ (anta, you — masculine), أنتِ (anti, you — feminine), هو (huwa, he), هي (hiya, she). Note that أنتَ and أنتِ are spelled almost identically — the difference is in the short vowel at the end (fatḥa for masculine, kasra for feminine), which is often not written. In context, you determine which 'you' is meant by the gender of the person being addressed. The pronoun أنا (I) does not change for gender — both men and women say أنا.
sections.tip: Think of it this way: أنا (I) is universal, أنتَ/أنتِ (you) splits by gender, and هو/هي (he/she) splits by gender. First person has no gender distinction, but second and third person always do.

examples.en: I am a student (female speaking) — أنا stays the same, the noun changes
You are a teacher (to a male)
You are a teacher (to a female)

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'I' in Arabic?
exercises.explanation: أنا (anā) means 'I' and is used by both males and females. It is the only singular pronoun that does not change for gender.

exercises.prompt: Which pronoun would you use to say 'you' to a female friend?
exercises.explanation: أنتِ (anti, with kasra) is the feminine 'you'. أنتَ (anta, with fatḥa) is the masculine 'you'. Arabic always distinguishes gender when addressing someone directly.
هي (hiya) means 'she'. هو (huwa) means 'he'. These are the third-person singular pronouns.

sections.title: Plural Pronouns
sections.explanation: Arabic has separate plural pronouns and also distinguishes a dual form (for exactly two people), though the dual is less common in everyday speech. The main plural pronouns are: نحن (naḥnu, we), أنتم (antum, you all — masculine or mixed group), أنتنّ (antunna, you all — feminine only), هم (hum, they — masculine or mixed group), هنّ (hunna, they — feminine only). In practice, أنتم and هم are used for any mixed-gender group (even if only one male is present). The purely feminine forms أنتنّ and هنّ are used only when the group is entirely female. Modern Standard Arabic also has dual pronouns — أنتما (you two) and هما (they two) — but these are rare in spoken Arabic.
sections.tip: In everyday usage, you will mostly use نحن (we), أنتم (you all), and هم (they). The feminine-only plurals أنتنّ and هنّ are used much less frequently. When in doubt, أنتم and هم cover mixed groups.

examples.en: You all are teachers (to a male or mixed group)
They are in the house (males or mixed group)
They (all female) are in the school

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'we' in Arabic?
exercises.explanation: نحن (naḥnu) means 'we'. Like أنا (I), it does not change for gender — both men and women use نحن.

exercises.prompt: If you are addressing a group of men and women, which 'you' do you use?
exercises.explanation: أنتم (antum) is used for a masculine or mixed-gender group. أنتنّ (antunna) is only used when the entire group is female.
هم (hum) means 'they' and is used for groups of males or mixed-gender groups. The all-female equivalent is هنّ (hunna).

sections.title: Using Pronouns in Sentences
sections.explanation: In Arabic, a simple sentence with a pronoun and a noun (like 'I am a teacher') does not require a verb 'to be' in the present tense. This is called a nominal sentence (جملة اسمية). You simply place the pronoun next to the predicate: أنا معلّم (I [am] a teacher), هو طبيب (He [is] a doctor). Arabic is also a pro-drop language — the verb conjugation already indicates the subject, so pronouns are often omitted when the subject is clear from the verb form. Pronouns are used mainly for emphasis or clarity. When a pronoun IS used with a verb, it adds emphasis: أنا أدرس (I study — with emphasis on 'I').
sections.tip: Arabic does not use a verb for 'is/am/are' in the present tense. Just put the pronoun and the description side by side: هي جميلة = She [is] beautiful. No verb needed!

examples.en: I am an engineer — no verb 'am' needed
She is beautiful — no verb 'is' needed

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'He is a doctor' in Arabic?
exercises.explanation: In Arabic present-tense nominal sentences, there is no verb 'to be'. You simply say هو طبيب (literally 'He doctor'). No equivalent of 'is' is needed.

exercises.prompt: Why are pronouns sometimes omitted in Arabic sentences?
options: Because pronouns are only used in formal writing
Because Arabic does not have pronouns
Because the verb conjugation already indicates the subject
exercises.explanation: Arabic is a pro-drop language. Each verb form is conjugated differently for each person, so the subject is already encoded in the verb. Pronouns are added only for emphasis or when the subject is ambiguous.

exercises.prompt: What kind of sentence is أنا طالب (I am a student)?
options: A nominal sentence (جملة اسمية) — no verb needed
exercises.explanation: أنا طالب is a nominal sentence (جملة اسمية), which consists of a subject and a predicate without any verb. In Arabic, present-tense 'is/am/are' is expressed through this structure — no verb required.
